passages.explanation: Jesus begins to teach again by the lake. The crowd is so large he gets into a boat and sits in it on the lake, while the people are on the shore. He teaches them many things by parables. Listen! A farmer goes out to sow. Some seed falls on the path — birds eat it. Some falls on rocky ground — it springs up quickly but withers when the sun scorches it because it has no root. Some falls among thorns, which choke it so it produces nothing. Some falls on good soil — it comes up, grows, and produces a crop, some thirty, some sixty, some a hundred times what was sown. Then Jesus says: whoever has ears to hear, let them hear. The parable is deceptively simple. Four types of reception, one type of seed. Jesus ends with a call to active listening — the parable is itself a test of what kind of soil you are.

When Jesus is alone with the Twelve and the others around him, they ask about the parables. He tells them: the secret of the kingdom of God has been given to you. But to those outside, everything is said in parables so that they may be ever seeing but never perceiving, and ever hearing but never understanding — otherwise they might turn and be forgiven. He quotes Isaiah 6, where the prophet's message actually hardens hearts. Then he asks: don't you understand this parable? How will you understand any parable? He explains: the farmer sows the word. The path is those who hear, then Satan immediately takes away the word. The rocky ground is those who receive it with joy but have no root — when trouble or persecution comes because of the word, they quickly fall away. The thorns are those who hear the word, but the worries of this life, the deceitfulness of wealth, and the desires for other things come in and choke the word, making it unfruitful. The good soil is those who hear the word, accept it, and produce a crop — thirty, sixty, or even a hundred times what was sown. Even the good soil varies in yield. Jesus doesn't demand identical results — he expects growth.

Jesus says: do you bring in a lamp to put it under a bowl or a bed? Instead, don't you put it on its stand? Whatever is hidden is meant to be disclosed, and whatever is concealed is meant to be brought out into the open. If anyone has ears to hear, let them hear. Consider carefully what you hear. With the measure you use, it will be measured to you — and even more. Whoever has will be given more; whoever does not have, even what they have will be taken from them. The parables that hide truth now will reveal it later. The concealment is temporary — everything hidden is destined for disclosure. And engagement with what you receive determines whether you get more or lose what you have.

Jesus says: this is what the kingdom of God is like. A man scatters seed on the ground. Night and day, whether he sleeps or gets up, the seed sprouts and grows, though he doesn't know how. All by itself the soil produces grain — first the stalk, then the head, then the full kernel in the head. As soon as the grain is ripe, he puts the sickle to it, because the harvest has come. This parable is unique to Mark. The farmer plants and harvests but doesn't cause the growth — it happens 'all by itself.' The kingdom advances by a power that operates independently of human effort or understanding. The farmer's job is to sow and then to harvest. The growth is God's.

Jesus asks: what shall we say the kingdom of God is like? What parable shall we use to describe it? It's like a mustard seed, which is the smallest of all seeds you plant in the ground. Yet when planted, it grows and becomes the largest of all garden plants, with such big branches that the birds can perch in its shade. With many similar parables Jesus speaks the word to them, as much as they can understand. He doesn't say anything to them without using a parable. But when he's alone with his own disciples, he explains everything. The kingdom starts impossibly small and becomes disproportionately large. Jesus calibrates his teaching — parables for the crowds, explanations for the inner circle. Understanding is progressive, not instant.

That day when evening comes, Jesus says: let's go over to the other side. Leaving the crowd behind, they take him along, just as he is, in the boat. Other boats are with him. A furious squall comes up, and the waves break over the boat so that it's nearly swamped. Jesus is in the stern, sleeping on a cushion. The disciples wake him: teacher, don't you care if we drown? He gets up, rebukes the wind, and says to the waves: quiet! Be still! The wind dies down and it's completely calm. He says to the disciples: why are you so afraid? Do you still have no faith? They're terrified and ask each other: who is this? Even the wind and the waves obey him! The disciples' question is the right one — and it's the question Mark wants every reader to answer. Jesus sleeps through a storm that terrifies experienced fishermen, then silences it with a word. In the Old Testament, only God commands the sea.
